1: The inner east gate on Sabbath and New Moon: Sha'ar hechatzer hapniymit hapaneh qadim yihyeh sagur sheshet yemey hama'aseh ('the gate of the inner court facing east shall be shut the six working days'). The same east gate that was discussed in Ezekiel 44 in relation to the prince's access is here given its regular weekly schedule. On Sabbaths and New Moons it is opened (uveyom haShabbat yipatach uveyom hachodesh yipatach) — the elevated sacred days receive elevated architectural access. The gate's weekly opening cycle creates a physical expression of the sacred calendar: the gate itself marks time by opening and closing with the rhythm of holy days.
2: The prince's Sabbath and New Moon ritual: Uvah hanasi derek ulam shaar mihutz ('the prince shall enter by way of the porch of the gate from outside'): the nasi enters through the east inner gate porch, stands by the gate doorpost, watches the priests offer his burnt offering and peace offering, and then prostrates himself (vehishtachavah al-miftan hasha'ar veyatza). The specific exit route — the way he came — is specified. This ritual entry and exit pattern is significant: the nasi participates in the Sabbath worship through observation and prostration at the threshold, the maximum access permitted him short of entering the inner court.
3: The congregation worships at the outer gate: The people of the land (am ha'aretz) prostrate themselves at the entrance to this gate before YHWH on Sabbaths and new moons. The layered worship positions are architecturally enacted: the priests are inside offering at the altar, the prince is at the inner gate porch, the congregation is at the outer gate entrance — each group is as close to YHWH as their status permits. This is a spatial liturgy: the worshipping community is arranged in concentric rings of proximity that express the holiness gradient in real time.
4: Sabbath burnt offering: six lambs and a ram: Veha'olah asher-yaqriv hanasi lADONAI beyom haShabbat shishah kevasim temimim ve'ayil tamim. The Sabbath offering of six lambs and one ram from the nasi differs from the Numbers 28:9-10 prescription (two lambs for the Sabbath). The heightened offering in Ezekiel reflects either a more elaborate future liturgy or the increased responsibility of the nasi as the sanctuary's fiscal patron. The Sabbath is the temporal center of the weekly cycle just as the inner sanctuary is the spatial center of the temple complex — appropriate that the prince's highest weekly sacrifice occurs on the Sabbath.
5: Grain offering proportions: An ephah per ram and whatever he can afford (matnat yado) per lamb, and a hin of oil per ephah. The flexible provision for the lamb grain offering (matnat yado = 'the gift of his hand' = as much as he can give) introduces a pastoral accommodation: the lamb offering's grain accompaniment scales with capacity. This is not a reduction of the standard but a recognition that multiplied lamb offerings might require flexible grain quantities. The ram's grain offering remains fixed at one ephah — only the more numerous lamb portion is made flexible.
6: New Moon offering: a bull, six lambs, and a ram: The New Moon (rosh chodesh) offering adds a bull to the Sabbath formula, making the New Moon the higher of the two regular cycles. This matches the Numbers 28:11-15 principle that the New Moon offering exceeds the Sabbath offering in number. The bull represents a step up from the Sabbath lamb-and-ram combination, reflecting the New Moon's status as a monthly community feast (1 Samuel 20:5, 18; Amos 8:5; Isaiah 1:13-14). The monthly rhythm of the new moon created a punctuation mark in the calendar requiring a more generous public offering.
7: New Moon grain offerings: An ephah for the bull, an ephah for the ram, whatever he can afford for the lambs, and a hin of oil per ephah. The grain proportions replicate the Passover grain formula (Ezekiel 45:24) — an ephah per major animal (bull/ram) and flexible for lambs. The consistent use of the hin of oil per ephah creates a standard oil-to-grain ratio across all the major offering occasions. This standardization prevents arbitrary escalation or reduction of the offering components — the prince cannot substitute different ratios even if he provides more expensive base animals.
8: The prince's entry and exit pattern: Uvevo'o hanasi derek ulam hasha'ar yavo uvederekh yetze ('when the prince enters, he shall enter by the way of the gate porch, and by the same way he shall go out'). The reciprocal entry-exit pattern for ordinary days. The gate-protocol has a theological logic: entering and exiting by the same route prevents the prince from "cutting through" the sacred space — each traversal is intentional and controlled. The prince's movements through the sanctuary are liturgical acts, not casual transits.
9: The congregation's entrance and exit pattern: Uvevo bam ha'aretz lifney YHWH bamoadim haba derech sha'ar tzafon lehishtachavot yetze derech sha'ar negev ('when the people of the land come before YHWH at the appointed feasts, whoever enters by the north gate to worship shall go out by the south gate'). The congregation uses a different traffic flow: north-in, south-out (or vice versa). This prevents crowding and creates an orderly flow. The requirement that no one returns by the same entrance gate applies to the entire congregation: the one-way flow ensures that the sacred space is traversed in a directed manner — the congregation moves through the sacred zone rather than oscillating in it.
10: The prince moves with the people: Vehanasi betocham bevo'am yavo uvetseatam yetze'u ('and the prince among them — when they enter he shall enter, and when they go out he shall go out'). The nasi does not have a private worship schedule separate from the congregation: he enters and exits with the people, sharing their liturgical movement. This is the democratic constraint on the nasi's elevated liturgical status: he has special gate access and special offering responsibilities, but he moves with his people rather than independently of them. The prince's solidarity with the congregation is architecturally enforced.
11: Feast and festival grain offerings: At feasts and appointed times, the grain offering shall be an ephah per bull and per ram, and whatever he can afford per lamb, and a hin of oil per ephah. This verse establishes the consistency of the offering formula across all the major occasions: Passover (Ezekiel 45:24), New Moon (Ezekiel 46:7), Feast of Tabernacles (Ezekiel 45:25), and now general feasts — all use the same ephah-per-large-animal and hin-per-ephah formula. The consistency is the point: the sacred calendar operates with uniform mathematical proportions, creating predictability and preventing ad-hoc variation.
12: Freewill offerings: the east gate opened: Vechi ya'aseh hanasi nedavah olah o shelamim nedavah lADONAI ('and when the prince offers a freewill offering (nedavah), whether a burnt offering or a peace offering, as a freewill offering to YHWH'). The nedavah (freewill/spontaneous offering) from Leviticus 22:18-25 is here given a special architectural provision: the east inner gate is opened for it, as on Sabbath and New Moon. The spontaneous devotion of the prince triggers the same gate-opening as the fixed calendar — YHWH receives individual devotion with the same welcome as liturgical obligation. The gate is opened and shut after the prince exits, maintaining its normally-closed status.
13: The daily morning lamb: Keves ben-shenato tamim ya'aseh olah layom tamid ('a yearling lamb without blemish shall be offered as a burnt offering to YHWH daily — the tamid'). The tamid (continual/regular) offering of one lamb each morning continues from Exodus 29:38-42 and Numbers 28:3-8, which prescribed two lambs daily (morning and evening). Ezekiel's restoration version has only the morning lamb with no evening companion — a simplification or possibly a transitional arrangement. The morning lamb anchors every day in sacrificial worship: the first act of each day is a burnt offering to YHWH.
14: Daily grain and oil for the tamid: 1/6 of an ephah of grain and 1/3 of a hin of oil to moisten the flour — a perpetual grain offering, always (chok olam tamid). The perpetual nature of the daily offering system is emphasized by the doubled absolute: chok olam (eternal statute) and tamid (continual). The consistency of the offering formula ensures that the daily sacrifice is never a matter of mood or circumstance — it is an institutional obligation as permanent as the covenant itself. The grain and oil proportions for the morning tamid are specified to prevent arbitrary variation.
15: Daily lamb, grain, and oil — morning by morning: Ya'asu et-hakeves veha-minchah veha-shemen baboqer baboqer olat tamid ('they shall prepare the lamb, the grain offering, and the oil morning by morning as a perpetual burnt offering'). The threefold baboqer baboqer (morning by morning) echoes the Exodus manna-gathering formula (baboqer baboqer, Exodus 16:21) — each new day begins with YHWH's provision meeting Israel's need and Israel's offering meeting YHWH's worship. The daily sacrificial rhythm is Israel's covenant heartbeat: as long as the tamid burns, the covenant is active.
16: The prince's inheritance law: sons: Ki yiten hanasi mattanah le'achad mibanav nachalatov hi livanaiv tihyeh ('if the prince gives a gift from his inheritance to one of his sons, it shall be his — it belongs to his sons as a hereditary possession'). The nasi may permanently alienate portions of his land allocation to his sons. This is a hereditary gift within the family — consistent with the normal inheritance laws. The permanent transfer to sons is the only form of permanent alienation permitted; all other transfers are temporary (reverts at jubilee). The son-permanent/servant-temporary distinction maintains the tribal integrity of land distribution across generations.
17: Gifts to servants revert at the Jubilee: Ve'im yiten mattanah me'nachalato le'achad me'avadav ('but if he gives a gift from his inheritance to one of his servants'): the servant's gift is temporary, reverting in the year of Jubilee. Halibanaiv tihyeh nachalatam hi ('for it is their inheritance — it belongs to his sons'). The Jubilee principle from Leviticus 25:8-17 governs the prince's gift-giving: he cannot permanently impoverish the tribal allotments by giving them to non-tribal members. The annual accumulation that led to the economic disparities condemned in Isaiah 5:8 ('woe to those who join house to house') is structurally prevented by the Jubilee reversion.
18: The prince shall not take the people's inheritance: Velo yiqach hanasi menahalt ha'am lehodiham me'achutzatam ('the prince shall not take any part of the inheritance of the people, pushing them out of their property'). This verse explicitly prohibits the Naboth's Vineyard scenario (1 Kgs 21) — royal land acquisition by coercion. The nasi may only give from his own allotted territory, not from the people's tribal holdings. Lemen lo yafutzu ammi ish me'achutzato ('so that my people shall not be scattered each from his property'): the connection between land tenure and communal stability is explicit — dispossession leads to dispersal, which is the covenant-curse of exile. The restoration order structurally prevents the conditions that led to the pre-exilic social catastrophe.
19: Ezekiel is led by the gate entrance to the holy chambers of the priests (lishkot haqodesh asher lak-kohanim) facing north. In the westernmost part, there is a place at the two ends. The priestly cooking areas are at the extreme west end of the two side chambers — physically separated from the sacrificial preparation areas to prevent inadvertent transfer of holiness from guilt/sin offerings to other foods. The spatial organization of cooking zones reflects the theological organization of holiness categories: certain offering types are too charged to be handled in proximity to general food preparation.
20: Where priests cook the guilt and sin offerings: Zeh hamaqom asher yevashelu sham hakohanim et-he'asham ve'et-hachatat ('this is the place where the priests shall boil the guilt offering and the sin offering'). The inner cooking courts are the only places where the asham (guilt offering) and chatat (sin offering) may be cooked — both are holy-of-holies offerings (Leviticus 6:25-29; Ezekiel 7:6-7) that cannot be brought into the outer court lest they consecrate the people there. The principle: lebilti hotzia el-hechatzer hachutzah liqaddesh et-ha'am ('so as not to bring them out to the outer court and consecrate the people'). Holy-of-holies holiness is contagious — it would "charge" anyone who touches the place where these offerings were cooked.
21: The outer court has four corner courts, each 40 by 30 cubits, and they are of equal size — the four corners of the outer court. The symmetric placement of the four cooking courts at the corners creates a perfectly balanced outer court layout. This architectural symmetry is itself a theological statement: the provision for ordinary people's cooking of their peace offerings (as opposed to the inner holy-of-holies offerings) is given equal weight at all four compass points. No direction of the outer court is prioritized over another for ordinary communal meal-preparation.
22: The four corner courts are enclosed courts, 40 by 30 each. A row of masonry (tira'ot) is built around the inner edge of each. These small enclosed spaces function as roofed or semi-roofed kitchens — the architectural detail of the surrounding masonry suggests permanent cooking facilities rather than temporary arrangements. The excavation of Iron Age installations at temple sites in the Levant (Tel Arad, Tel Dan) reveals permanent cooking and offering-preparation infrastructure as a standard feature of Israelite cultic sites, confirming the practical necessity of what Ezekiel describes.
23: The hearths under the surrounding ledge: Umesharetin tachat hamisgeret saviv saviv ('and places for boiling beneath the rows around'). The cooking hearths are built beneath a surrounding ledge — presumably a protective overhang that keeps weather out while allowing smoke to escape. The technical detail of permanent fixed hearths reflects the practical reality that large-scale sacrificial preparation requires substantial, permanent cooking infrastructure. The daily tamid sacrifice plus festival offerings plus peace offerings from thousands of worshippers creates an enormous food-preparation demand that the temple's outer court must accommodate.
24: The cooking courts for the people's offerings: Va'yomer elay elleh beit hamevashellim asher yevashelu sham mesharetey habayit et-zevach ha'am ('and he said to me: these are the boiling houses where the temple ministers shall boil the sacrifice of the people'). The final verse of Ezekiel 46 closes the architectural tour with a practical detail: the outer court cooking areas serve the people's peace offerings, which are eaten by the worshipper and their family (Leviticus 7:11-17). The restoration temple provides not just a place for priests to offer and YHWH to dwell, but a full communal meal infrastructure — the sacred feast that bonds Israel to YHWH and to each other is physically built into the temple's ground plan.
